title: The role of the intercostal muscles in restricted breathing patterns
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
The muscles you never think about 

 I spent years trying to breathe better. I did the breathing exercises. I downloaded the apps. I sat in meditation circles...
metaDescription:
The muscles you never think about 

 I spent years trying to breathe better. I did the breathing exercises. I downloaded the apps. I sat in meditation cir...
htmlContent:
The muscles you never think about

I spent years trying to breathe better. I did the breathing exercises. I downloaded the apps. I sat in meditation circles where everyone talked about "belly breathing" like it was the answer to everything. And still, my chest stayed tight. My shoulders crept up toward my ears. My ribs felt locked in place, like someone had wrapped a belt around my torso and cinched it one notch too tight.

Here's the thing. Nobody told me about the intercostal muscles. These are the small muscles between your ribs, the ones that help your rib cage expand and contract with each breath. And when you've lived through trauma, when your nervous system has been stuck in survival mode for months or years or decades, these muscles learn to hold. They learn to brace. They forget how to let go.

I want you to put your hand on your rib cage right now. Just for a second. Feel the space between your ribs. Notice if there's any movement when you breathe. Notice if your ribs feel stiff, like they don't want to budge. That's your intercostals talking. They're telling you something about what your body has been through.

What the intercostals actually do

There are two layers of intercostal muscles. The external intercostals help you inhale. They lift the rib cage up and out, creating space for your lungs to fill. The internal intercostals help you exhale. They pull the ribs down and in, pushing air out. They work together, a constant back and forth, like a bellows.

But here's where it gets interesting. When you're under threat, your body prioritizes protection over breathing. Your diaphragm tightens. Your abdominal muscles clench. And your intercostals lock into a pattern of shallow, rapid breathing that never fully relaxes. This isn't a choice. It's a reflex. Your nervous system is trying to keep you safe, even if that safety comes at the cost of your breath.

Look, I know this is heavy material. And I know there's a part of you that might be thinking "this is too much" or "I can't handle this" or "maybe I should just close this tab." That part is trying to protect you. And that's okay. But I want you to know that you can handle this. You've already handled worse. The fact that you're here, reading this, means your survival system did its job. You made it.

What we're doing here isn't about reliving the past. It's about understanding it well enough to stop being controlled by it. There's a difference between being trapped in a memory and looking at it from a safe distance. You get to choose how close you get. You get to choose when to step back. That choice - that agency - is itself a form of healing.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, has written extensively about how trauma lives in the body's physical structures. He talks about how the breath becomes a prisoner of the nervous system's threat responses. And he's right. When I finally understood that my tight intercostals weren't a failure of willpower or a lack of meditation practice, I started to feel something shift. Not immediately. Not dramatically. But a small crack of compassion for what my body had been doing to protect me.

Think about it. Your intercostals have been working overtime for years, holding your rib cage in a position of readiness. They've been waiting for the next threat. They've been bracing for impact. And they don't know how to stop. Because your nervous system hasn't gotten the message that the danger is over.

The connection between ribs and fear

There's a reason why panic attacks feel like someone is sitting on your chest. Your intercostals are involved. When fear hits, your body goes into fight or flight. Your breathing becomes fast and shallow. Your intercostals work harder but with less range of motion. You're taking in air, but not enough. You're exhaling, but not fully. It's a cycle that feeds itself.

Let me ask you something. When was the last time you felt truly relaxed in your body? Not distracted. Not numbed out. Actually relaxed. If you can't remember, that tells you something important. Your nervous system may have forgotten what rest feels like. It may have decided, somewhere along the way, that rest is dangerous.

Deb Dana talks about this - how the dorsal vagal state can look like relaxation from the outside but actually feels like collapse on the inside. There's a difference between choosing to rest and being unable to move. There's a difference between peace and shutdown. Your body knows the difference, even if the people around you can't tell.

Notice your hands right now. Are they clenched? Open? Cold? Warm? Your hands tell a story about your autonomic state. Cold hands often mean sympathetic activation - blood has moved to the core, preparing for threat. Warm, relaxed hands suggest ventral vagal engagement - safety. It's a small thing to notice. But small things are where healing lives.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, explains this through the lens of the vagus nerve. The vagus nerve runs through your chest and abdomen, and it's intimately connected to your breathing muscles. When your vagal tone is low, when your nervous system is dysregulated, your intercostals can't access the full range of motion they need for calm, rhythmic breathing. They're stuck in a pattern of vigilance.

I remember sitting in a therapist's office years ago, trying to do a simple breathing exercise. Inhale for four counts. Hold for four. Exhale for four. And I couldn't do it. My chest wouldn't expand. My ribs wouldn't move. I felt like a failure. But now I understand that my intercostals were doing exactly what they'd been trained to do. They were keeping me safe. They were holding the line.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. He writes about how trauma reshapes the body's physical responses, including the breath. Reading it helped me understand that my tight chest wasn't a personal failing. It was a biological adaptation.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger by Peter Levine. He gets into the specifics of how animals release trauma through the body, and how humans can learn from that. It's not a quick fix. But it's real.

I want to talk about something that doesn't get enough attention. The way chronic stress changes your relationship with pain. When your nervous system has been in overdrive for years, your pain threshold shifts. Things that shouldn't hurt, hurt. Things that should hurt, don't register at all. Your body's volume knob is broken.

This is what the research on central sensitization shows. The spinal cord and brain start amplifying pain signals. Fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue, irritable bowel - these aren't imaginary. They're the body's honest response to a nervous system that never got to complete its stress cycle. The energy that was meant for fight or flight got trapped. And trapped energy has to go somewhere.

Sit with that for a moment. If you've been told your pain isn't real, if you've been dismissed by doctors, if you've started to doubt your own body - I want you to know something. Your body isn't lying. It's speaking the only language it has. The question isn't whether the pain is real. The question is what your body is trying to tell you through it.

How to start working with your intercostals

I'm not going to tell you to do deep breathing exercises. Not yet. Because if your intercostals are locked, forcing deep breaths can actually make things worse. You might trigger a panic response. You might feel like you're suffocating. Your nervous system will interpret the effort as a threat.

Instead, start with awareness. Just notice. Put your hands on your ribs. Breathe normally. Don't try to change anything. Just feel the movement, or the lack of it. Notice where your breath stops. Notice where your ribs feel stuck. That's your starting point.

Then try something small. Exhale completely. Let all the air out. Then wait. Don't inhale. Just wait until your body naturally wants to take a breath. That pause, that moment of emptiness, can sometimes allow your intercostals to reset. It's not a technique. It's an invitation.

Something happens in the body when safety has been absent for a long time. The muscles forget how to relax. Not metaphorically - literally. The fascia tightens. The diaphragm restricts. The psoas - that deep hip flexor that connects your spine to your legs - stays chronically contracted, ready to run or curl into a ball at any moment.

Bessel van der Kolk's research on yoga and trauma showed something worth noticing. Traumatized people who practiced yoga showed changes in their brain scans - specifically in the insula, the part of the brain responsible for interoception, for feeling what's happening inside your own body. Many trauma survivors have learned to disconnect from body sensation entirely. It was too dangerous to feel. Yoga - gentle, trauma-informed yoga - helped them come back.

I'm not saying yoga fixes everything. I'm saying the body needs to be included in healing. You can't think your way out of something that lives below thought. You have to feel your way through it. Slowly. With support. At your own pace. There's no rushing this.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs. The gentle pressure can help your intercostals feel supported, which sometimes allows them to relax. It's not a cure. But it can create a sense of safety that makes breathing easier.

A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs. When your nervous system is overwhelmed by sound, your intercostals can tighten even more. Reducing auditory input can sometimes help your whole system settle, including those muscles between your ribs.

Here's something wild. Your body can hold a trauma response for decades without you knowing it. You might think you've "dealt with" something because you can talk about it without crying. But talking about it uses the left hemisphere - language, logic, narrative. The trauma might still be living in your right hemisphere, in your body, in your implicit memory.

Pat Ogden's work on sensorimotor psychotherapy addresses exactly this gap. She noticed that clients could have years of talk therapy and still be triggered by the same things. Because the body hadn't been included in the conversation. The body was still bracing, still guarding, still waiting for the other shoe to drop.

Try this. Stand up for a moment if you can. Feel your feet on the ground. Now push gently against a wall with both hands. Feel the resistance. Feel your own strength pushing back. That's your body remembering that it can take action. That it's not helpless. That it has agency. Sometimes the body needs to complete a movement it was never allowed to make. That's not weird. That's healing.

Stay with me here. This work is slow. It's not linear. Some days your ribs will feel open and your breath will flow. Other days you'll feel like you're back at square one, chest tight, shoulders hunched. That's not regression. That's your body doing its job. It's protecting you from something it hasn't fully processed yet.

I've been working with my intercostals for years now. Some days I can feel them release. A small shift. A little more space between my ribs. Other days they hold tight, and I have to remind myself that this is not a failure. This is information. My body is telling me something about what I'm feeling, what I'm carrying, what I haven't been able to let go of.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did your breath change when you read that? Did your ribs shift? Did you feel a tightness somewhere you hadn't noticed before? That's the intercostals talking. They've been talking this whole time. You just didn't know how to listen.
